questions: A Mac user is troubleshooting several issues at once: they’ve forgotten their account credentials, want to reinstall the operating system without losing personal files, need an automatic backup solution, and also want to ensure their computer is running the latest system version. In addition, they are looking for official guides for a specific macOS release, community discussion forums, direct technical assistance options, and ways to manage their Apple account or book in-person service. Identify the official Apple tools, features, and support channels that address each of these needs, and explain the purpose of each one.

When exploring resources related to getting started with a Mac and seeking further help through Apple’s official website, users can navigate to (1) a comprehensive navigational page that organizes all of Apple’s products, services, retail options, and support resources into structured categories, and (2) a legal policy page that governs use of the website, including rules about automated access, intellectual property, liability limits, dispute resolution, and account security. Identify these two distinct pages and explain their primary purposes. Then, drawing from both pages, summarize: (a) how Apple organizes its major product families and support resources in the navigational structure, and (b) the key legal provisions concerning prohibited site access methods, limitations of liability (including any monetary cap), governing law and venue, and user responsibilities for account security.

When exploring official support resources for macOS on Apple’s website, a user can navigate through the footer to find separate documents governing (1) the structure of the company’s website and product ecosystem, (2) the rules for returning products purchased in the United States, and (3) the legal terms that apply simply by using the website. Identify these three distinct documents and explain their primary purposes. Then, compare and synthesize the following details across all of them: the time limits or deadlines they impose (such as return windows or claim periods), any monetary liability caps or refund conditions they specify, and key restrictions they place on user behavior (for example, limitations on automated access, resale, or product returns). Your answer should clearly distinguish which provisions belong to which document while showing how they collectively govern browsing, purchasing, and interacting with Apple’s online and retail services.

When exploring official online resources related to setting up and using a Mac computer, you can navigate to broader corporate and legal information pages maintained by the same company. Identify the three distinct types of pages that respectively: (1) provide a comprehensive navigational directory of the company’s products, services, retail options, and support resources; (2) outline the company’s United States return, refund, price adjustment, and order policies for hardware purchases; and (3) govern a user’s legal rights, responsibilities, and limitations when accessing and using the company’s main website. For each of these three pages, explain their primary purpose and summarize key provisions, including any specific time limits, monetary thresholds, user obligations, liability caps, governing law clauses, or notable restrictions described on each page.

rounds.question: A Mac user is troubleshooting several issues at once: they’ve forgotten their account credentials, want to reinstall the operating system without losing personal files, need an automatic backup solution, and also want to ensure their computer is running the latest system version. In addition, they are looking for official guides for a specific macOS release, community discussion forums, direct technical assistance options, and ways to manage their Apple account or book in-person service. Identify the official Apple tools, features, and support channels that address each of these needs, and explain the purpose of each one.
checklist_width: Reset Apple ID password – explains account credential recovery process
macOS Recovery – used to reinstall macOS without deleting personal data
Time Machine – automatic backup solution using an external storage device
Upgrade macOS process – how to keep a Mac up to date with the latest version
macOS Sonoma User Guide – official guide to features and usage
Apple Communities – forum for asking and answering user questions
Get Support – portal to find official technical assistance options
Apple Support app – personalized support access for Apple products
AppleCare Mac Support – warranty and extended service coverage information
Genius Bar – in-person technical service booking option
Manage Your Apple Account – account management portal
Downloads and Software Updates / Product Manuals – official resource library
checklist_depth: Reset Apple ID password + matches the forgotten credential scenario
macOS Recovery + specifically enables OS reinstallation without erasing personal data
Time Machine + identified as Apple’s built-in backup feature for Mac
Upgrade macOS + official method to keep macOS current
macOS Sonoma User Guide + example of an official macOS version guide
Apple Communities + official peer-support discussion platform
Apple Support app and Get Support + official direct support channels
AppleCare Mac Support and Genius Bar + represent extended coverage and in-person repair options
Manage Your Apple Account + official account administration portal
rounds.rationale: The question uses a realistic troubleshooting scenario (Deep) to logically group together account recovery, OS reinstallation, backup, updating, documentation, and support needs without naming the resources directly. It then requires broad aggregation (Wide) across multiple official Apple tools, guides, community platforms, and service channels, compelling the respondent to identify and explain all relevant entities rather than relying on a single source.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: macOS Support Reset your Apple ID password If you forgot your Apple ID password, you can reset it and regain access to your account. Learn how to reset your password Reinstall
rounds.answer_a:
• Reset forgotten credentials: macOS Recovery’s Reset Password assistant lets you unlock a user account with your Apple ID or FileVault recovery key[1]. If you cannot recall the Apple ID itself, the iForgot/Account Recovery portal at iforgot.apple.com guides you through regaining access to the Apple ID and all linked services[1].

• Reinstall macOS without erasing files: In macOS Recovery choose “Reinstall macOS.” The installer replaces system components while leaving user data, apps, and settings intact, ideal for troubleshooting software problems[2].

• Automatic, continuous backup: Time Machine (built into macOS) performs hourly, daily, and weekly snapshots to an external disk or network volume and supports easy point-in-time restores or full system recovery[3].

• Keep the system current: Software Update in System Settings (or System Preferences) checks Apple’s servers and installs the latest macOS, security, and firmware updates, ensuring you have the newest features and protections[4].

• Official user guides: Apple publishes free, version-specific macOS User Guides on its support site and Apple Books, providing step-by-step instructions and screenshots for each release (e.g., “macOS Ventura User Guide”)[5].

• Community discussion forum: Apple Support Communities host peer-to-peer Q&A, searchable archives, and reputation-based expertise for all Apple products[6].

• Direct technical assistance: The Apple Support app (iOS/iPadOS) and support.apple.com offer live chat, phone callbacks, screen-sharing sessions, and mail-in repair logistics with Apple advisors[7].

• Account management & in-person service: The Apple ID website lets you change passwords, enable two-factor authentication, and view devices, while the “Make a Reservation” flow on support.apple.com/repair books Genius Bar or Apple Authorized Service Provider appointments for hands-on diagnostics and repairs[8].

## References
1. https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT202860  
2. https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204904  
3. https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201250  
4. https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201541  
5. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac  
6. https://discussions.apple.com  
7. https://apps.apple.com/us/app/apple-support/id1130498044  
8. https://support.apple.com/repair
rounds.answer_b:
Apple provides a comprehensive ecosystem of tools, features, and support channels to address each of these needs:

**Forgotten Account Credentials:** **Apple ID Account Recovery** (iforgot.apple.com) allows users to reset their Apple ID password via email, security questions, or two-factor authentication. For Mac login passwords, **macOS Recovery Mode** (Command+R at startup) offers a **Reset Password** utility to regain access to local accounts [1][2].

**Reinstalling macOS Without Losing Files:** **macOS Recovery** (accessed via Command+R on Intel Macs or holding the power button on Apple Silicon) includes a **Reinstall macOS** option that preserves personal files, apps, and settings while replacing system files. This performs a clean OS installation without erasing user data [3].

**Automatic Backup Solution:** **Time Machine**, Apple's built-in backup feature, automatically creates incremental backups of your entire system—including files, apps, and settings—to an external drive or network storage. It runs hourly, daily, and weekly backups, enabling full system restoration or individual file recovery [4].

**Latest System Version:** **Software Update**, found in **System Settings > General > Software Update**, checks for and installs the latest macOS version and security patches. Users can enable automatic updates to stay current [5].

**Official Guides for a Specific macOS Release:** Apple publishes detailed **macOS User Guides** on support.apple.com for each release (e.g., Sonoma, Sequoia) [6].

**Community Discussion Forums:** **Apple Support Communities** (discussions.apple.com) offer peer-to-peer troubleshooting and advice [7].

**Direct Technical Assistance:** Users can contact Apple via **Apple Support** (support.apple.com) through chat, phone, or the **Apple Support App** for personalized help [8].

**Managing Apple Account:** The **Apple ID account page** (appleid.apple.com) manages subscriptions, devices, and security settings [1].

**In-Person Service:** Users can book **Genius Bar appointments** at Apple Stores through the Apple Support App or Apple's website [9].

## References
[1] Apple, "Apple ID Support," https://support.apple.com/apple-id
[2] Apple, "Reset Mac Login Password," https://support.apple.com/en-us/102543
[3] Apple, "How to reinstall macOS," https://support.apple.com/en-us/101578
[4] Apple, "Back up your Mac with Time Machine," https://support.apple.com/en-us/104984
[5] Apple, "Update macOS on Mac," https://support.apple.com/en-us/106380
[6] Apple, "macOS User Guide," https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/
[7] Apple, "Apple Support Communities," https://discussions.apple.com/
[8] Apple, "Apple Support," https://support.apple.com/
[9] Apple, "Genius Bar Reservations," https://www.apple.com/retail/geniusbar/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the core Apple tools (Apple ID reset/iForgot, macOS Recovery, Time Machine, Software Update, macOS User Guide, Apple Support Communities, Apple Support app, Genius Bar, and Apple ID management portal). Neither hallucinated incorrect entities. 

Width/Completeness: Both covered most checklist items accurately. However, both omitted explicit mention of AppleCare Mac Support and the broader Downloads/Product Manuals library. Thus, neither achieves perfect WIDE coverage, but both are largely complete and factually correct.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides clearer section headers with bold labels tied directly to each user need, making it more scannable and aligned to the multi-part query. The structure closely mirrors the problem statement, improving usability. Agent A is solid but slightly less structured and explicit in mapping each need. 

Since both are accurate and similarly complete, Agent B wins on formatting, clarity, and overall user experience. Hence, B_BETTER with no hard checklist failure from Agent A.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: When exploring resources related to getting started with a Mac and seeking further help through Apple’s official website, users can navigate to (1) a comprehensive navigational page that organizes all of Apple’s products, services, retail options, and support resources into structured categories, and (2) a legal policy page that governs use of the website, including rules about automated access, intellectual property, liability limits, dispute resolution, and account security. Identify these two distinct pages and explain their primary purposes. Then, drawing from both pages, summarize: (a) how Apple organizes its major product families and support resources in the navigational structure, and (b) the key legal provisions concerning prohibited site access methods, limitations of liability (including any monetary cap), governing law and venue, and user responsibilities for account security.
checklist_width: Description of how Apple organizes product families (e.g., Mac, iPad, iPhone, Apple Watch, Apple Vision Pro, TV & Home, Entertainment, Accessories)
Inclusion of retail and support categories (e.g., Apple Store, AppleCare, Manuals, Support, Genius Bar, Order Status)
Explanation that automated tools like robots, spiders, scraping, or deep-linking are prohibited
Statement of limitation of liability including the monetary cap (greater of fees paid in prior six months or US$100)
Identification of governing law (United States and State of California) and venue (Santa Clara County, California, with EU consumer exception)
Explanation of user responsibility for maintaining Apple ID/account password confidentiality
checklist_depth: Apple Site Map page identified as the comprehensive navigational index organizing products, services, retail, and support links
Apple Terms of Use page identified as the legal agreement governing website access, automated restrictions, liability limits, dispute resolution, and account security obligations
rounds.rationale: The question uses the context of seeking help from Mac-related resources to logically lead to two foundational Apple website pages without naming them directly (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregating structured commercial/navigation information from the comprehensive index page and detailed legal, liability, and governance provisions from the legal agreement page (Wide aggregation across two distinct sources).
source_nodes: Legal - Website Terms of Use - Apple
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Apple Site Map About Apple Apple Leadership Career Opportunities Investors Contact Apple Legal Ethics & Compliance Choose Your Country or Region Product Feedback Apple Values
rounds.answer_a:
Apple’s “Sitemap” page (https://www.apple.com/sitemap/) is the master navigation hub that lays out every public-facing section of Apple’s web presence in an ordered, expandable list. Its purpose is to give visitors a single glance overview and direct links to all product families, services, retail programs, corporate information, and support portals, making discovery simple even for first-time users [1].

The “Website Terms of Use” page (https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html) is the legal contract governing visits to any apple.com domain. It sets the rules for acceptable use, protects Apple’s intellectual property, limits Apple’s liability, states the applicable law and dispute venue, and describes user obligations such as keeping account credentials safe [2].

a) Navigational structure on the Sitemap  
• Products are grouped by hardware lines (Mac, iPad, iPhone, Watch, Vision, TV & Home, AirPods).  
• Each group expands to sub-models (e.g., MacBook Air, iMac) plus “Compare,” “Accessories,” and “Explore Mac.”  
• Services and software are in separate clusters: Apple Music, TV+, Fitness+, Arcade, iCloud, Apple One, etc.  
• “Apple Store” covers retail options like online store, Apple Store app, financing, trade-in, order status, and certified refurbished.  
• “Support” branches into dedicated portals for every product line, along with general resources such as AppleCare+, manuals, downloads, Communities, and contact routes.  
• Corporate categories include “Apple Leadership,” “Investors,” “Ethics & Compliance,” “Events,” and “Job Opportunities” [1].

b) Key legal provisions in the Terms of Use  
• Prohibited automated access: Using “robots, spiders, crawlers, scraping tools or other automated means” to access or copy any portion of the Site is forbidden; users may not interfere with servers or networks or attempt to gain unauthorized access [2].  
• Limitation of liability: Apple disclaims indirect or consequential damages, and its aggregate liability for any claim “will in no event exceed one hundred U.S. dollars (US $100.00)” even if remedies fail of their essential purpose [2].  
• Governing law & venue: The agreement is governed by the laws of the State of California, U.S.A., without regard to conflict-of-law principles; any dispute must be filed exclusively in the state or federal courts located in Santa Clara County, California [2].  
• Account security: Users are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of their Apple ID and password and for all activities that occur under their account; they must notify Apple immediately of any unauthorized use [2].

## References  
1. Apple Sitemap – https://www.apple.com/sitemap/  
2. Apple Website Terms of Use – https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html
rounds.answer_b:
The two distinct pages are:

**1. Apple Site Map (apple.com/sitemap):** This comprehensive navigational page organizes Apple's entire web presence into structured categories, allowing users to quickly locate any product, service, or resource [1].

**2. Apple Terms of Use (apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html):** This legal policy page governs all use of Apple's website, establishing rules for access, intellectual property protections, liability limitations, and user obligations [2].

## (a) How Apple Organizes Products and Support Resources

The Apple Site Map arranges content into clearly defined categories. Major product families include **Mac**, **iPad**, **iPhone**, **Apple Watch**, **Apple TV**, and **AirPods**, each with subcategories for individual models, comparisons, and accessories [1]. Services such as **Apple Music**, **Apple TV+**, **iCloud**, **Apple Arcade**, and **Apple Pay** are grouped separately. The **Support** section links to resources including product manuals, macOS user guides, Apple Support Communities, AppleCare coverage, and Genius Bar reservations. Additional categories cover **Apple Store** (online and retail), **For Education**, **For Business**, **Apple Values** (accessibility, environment, privacy), and **Account** management for Apple ID [1]. This structured layout enables new Mac users to efficiently find getting-started guides alongside broader support channels.

## (b) Key Legal Provisions

**Prohibited Access Methods:** The Terms of Use explicitly forbid using robots, spiders, scrapers, or other automated means to access the site without Apple's prior written permission [2].

**Limitations of Liability:** Apple's total liability for any claims arising from site use is capped at **$100 USD**. Apple disclaims liability for indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages [2].

**Governing Law and Venue:** The Terms are governed by the laws of the **State of California**, excluding conflict-of-law provisions. Disputes must be brought in **Santa Clara County, California** courts, and users consent to personal jurisdiction there [2].

**Account Security:** Users are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of their account credentials and for all activities occurring under their account. Users must notify Apple immediately of any unauthorized use [2].

## References
[1] Apple, "Apple Site Map," https://www.apple.com/sitemap/
[2] Apple, "Apple Terms of Use," https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the two required pages — Apple’s Sitemap and the Website Terms of Use — so both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Both answers cover product organization, retail/support categories, automated access prohibitions, liability limits, governing law (California, Santa Clara County), and account security responsibilities. However, both omit an important liability nuance: the monetary cap is the greater of fees paid in the prior six months or US$100. Neither mentions the EU consumer exception to venue. That said, Agent A provides broader product-family coverage (including Vision and TV & Home), more granular retail detail (refurbished, financing, trade-in), and a more structured breakdown of the sitemap hierarchy. Agent B is accurate but slightly less comprehensive in product categorization and structural detail.

Presentation & UX: Both are well formatted and scannable, but Agent A delivers denser, more complete coverage of the navigational structure and corporate/support branches, making it more useful to a user seeking a holistic understanding.

Conclusion: Since both have minor completeness gaps but Agent A provides noticeably richer structural detail and product coverage, Agent A is better on user utility and comprehensiveness. Hence, [[A_BETTER]].

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.question: When exploring official support resources for macOS on Apple’s website, a user can navigate through the footer to find separate documents governing (1) the structure of the company’s website and product ecosystem, (2) the rules for returning products purchased in the United States, and (3) the legal terms that apply simply by using the website. Identify these three distinct documents and explain their primary purposes. Then, compare and synthesize the following details across all of them: the time limits or deadlines they impose (such as return windows or claim periods), any monetary liability caps or refund conditions they specify, and key restrictions they place on user behavior (for example, limitations on automated access, resale, or product returns). Your answer should clearly distinguish which provisions belong to which document while showing how they collectively govern browsing, purchasing, and interacting with Apple’s online and retail services.
checklist_width: Identification of the website-structure document’s purpose (comprehensive navigation/index of Apple products, services, and corporate information)
Identification of the U.S. product return window (14 days from receipt)
Conditions for returns (original condition, receipt, included parts/accessories, inspection, country-of-purchase limitation)
Price adjustment policy within 14 calendar days and exclusions (e.g., limited-time promotions)
Monetary liability cap in the website-use legal terms (greater of fees paid in prior six months or US$100)
One-year claim limitation period under the website-use legal terms
Prohibition on automated tools (e.g., robots, spiders, scraping) in the website-use legal terms
Restrictions on resale or order refusal under sales policy
Overall purpose of the website-use legal terms (governing access, intellectual property, liability, dispute resolution)
checklist_depth: Apple Site Map + Identified as the structured index accessible from the footer listing products, services, and corporate links
U.S. Sales and Refund Policy + Identified as governing 14-day returns, refunds, price protection, and sales conditions in the United States
Terms of Use for apple.com + Identified as legally binding conditions for site access, including liability cap and restrictions on automated access
rounds.rationale: The question anchors itself in the Mac support browsing context (Deep Logic) and requires identifying three different but related footer-linked documents without naming them directly. It then demands cross-document synthesis (Wide Logic): return deadlines and refund rules from the sales policy, liability caps and behavioral restrictions from the terms governing site use, and the structural role of the site-wide index. Answering fully requires aggregating and comparing provisions from all three sources rather than relying on a single document.

Legal - Website Terms of Use - Apple
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Apple Site Map About Apple Apple Leadership Career Opportunities Investors Contact Apple Legal Ethics & Compliance Choose Your Country or Region Product Feedback Apple Values
rounds.answer_a:
Apple Sitemap (https://www.apple.com/sitemap/) – a purely navigational index that lays out every top-level section of Apple’s web presence (Mac, iPhone, Services, Store, Support, corporate pages, etc.), letting visitors jump straight to any product family or help area [1].

Apple Sales & Refunds Policy – United States (https://www.apple.com/shop/help/sales_refunds) – the retail contract that explains how, when, and under what conditions customers may return, exchange, or obtain refunds for items bought from Apple’s U.S. online or retail stores, plus purchase-for-resale prohibitions and shipping terms [2].

Apple Website Terms of Use (https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html) – the legal agreement that applies simply by visiting or browsing any apple.com page, covering intellectual-property ownership, acceptable-use rules, liability limits, governing law, and account-security duties [3].

Comparison of key provisions

Time limits / deadlines  
• Sales & Refunds: standard 14-calendar-day window from the date you receive a product to return it for a full refund or exchange; gift recipients also have 14 days to request a gift card credit [2].  
• Terms of Use: any lawsuit or claim arising from site use must be filed within one (1) year after the cause of action accrues [3].  
• Sitemap: no temporal provisions; it is informational only [1].

Monetary caps / refund conditions  
• Sales & Refunds: refunds are limited to the product’s original purchase price (including applicable tax); Apple may deduct the value of missing accessories or diminish refunds for damaged or personalized items. Shipping fees are refunded only if the entire order is returned or if Apple shipped in error [2].  
• Terms of Use: Apple’s aggregate liability for all damages related to site use is expressly capped at US $100.00, even if any stated remedy fails of its essential purpose [3].  
• Sitemap: no liability or refund clauses [1].

Behavioral restrictions  
• Sales & Refunds: purchases are for end-user use only; “Apple reserves the right to refuse or cancel your order if it suspects you are purchasing for resale” and may limit quantities. Returned items must be in original packaging with serial number intact [2].  
• Terms of Use: forbids using “robots, spiders, crawlers, scraping tools, or other automated means” to access or copy the site, prohibits interfering with servers or attempting unauthorized access, and requires users to safeguard Apple ID credentials [3].  
• Sitemap: imposes no conduct rules; it merely reflects the site’s content hierarchy [1].

Collectively, the three documents govern every stage of a customer’s interaction: the Sitemap guides discovery, the Terms of Use set baseline rules for browsing, and the Sales & Refunds Policy controls post-purchase rights and obligations.

## References  
1. Apple Sitemap – https://www.apple.com/sitemap/  
2. Apple Sales & Refunds Policy (U.S.) – https://www.apple.com/shop/help/sales_refunds  
3. Apple Website Terms of Use – https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html
rounds.answer_b:
The three distinct documents are:

**1. Apple Site Map (apple.com/sitemap):** A comprehensive navigational page organizing Apple's entire product ecosystem, services, retail options, and support resources into structured categories, enabling users to efficiently locate any page across Apple's web presence [1].

**2. Apple Sales and Refund Policy (apple.com/shop/help/returns_refund):** This document governs product returns and refunds for purchases made through Apple's U.S. online and retail stores, specifying eligibility conditions, return windows, and refund procedures [2].

**3. Apple Terms of Use (apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html):** The legal agreement governing all use of Apple's website, addressing intellectual property, prohibited access methods, liability limitations, dispute resolution, and account responsibilities [3].

## Time Limits and Deadlines

The **Sales and Refund Policy** imposes a **14-calendar-day return window** from the date of product receipt for most items, with some exceptions like opened software and electronic gift cards [2]. The **Terms of Use** does not specify a return deadline but requires that any legal claims be brought within the applicable statute of limitations, governed by **California law** [3]. The **Site Map** imposes no deadlines, serving purely as a navigational tool [1].

## Monetary Caps and Refund Conditions

The **Terms of Use** caps Apple's total liability at **$100 USD** and disclaims all indirect, consequential, and punitive damages [3]. The **Sales and Refund Policy** provides full refunds to the original payment method for eligible returns in original condition, though items not returned in original condition may incur restocking deductions or be declined [2]. The **Site Map** contains no monetary provisions [1].

## Restrictions on User Behavior

The **Terms of Use** prohibits automated access via robots, spiders, or scrapers without written permission, and holds users responsible for account security and all activity under their credentials [3]. The **Sales and Refund Policy** restricts returns of opened software, limits quantities per household for certain products, prohibits commercial resale of purchased items, and may refuse returns that don't meet eligibility criteria [2]. The **Site Map** imposes no behavioral restrictions [1].

Collectively, these three documents govern how users **navigate** Apple's ecosystem, **purchase and return** products under defined conditions, and **legally interact** with Apple's digital services.

## References
[1] Apple, "Apple Site Map," https://www.apple.com/sitemap/
[2] Apple, "Returns & Refunds," https://www.apple.com/shop/help/returns_refund
[3] Apple, "Apple Terms of Use," https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the three required documents—Apple Site Map, U.S. Sales & Refund Policy, and Apple Terms of Use—so both pass the entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A includes the one-year claim limitation in the Terms of Use, which is explicitly required by the checklist. Agent B incorrectly states that claims are governed by the applicable statute of limitations under California law, omitting the contractual one-year limitation—this is a substantive checklist miss. Both agents mention the $100 liability cap but omit the fuller “greater of fees paid in prior six months or $100” formulation, and neither discusses the 14-day price adjustment policy in detail. However, Agent A still satisfies more checklist elements overall (including the one-year limitation and clearer refund-condition nuances).

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both are well-structured and readable, but Agent A provides slightly more precise comparisons (e.g., gift recipient window, shipping refund conditions, inspection/deduction nuances) and clearer synthesis of how the documents interact. Since both are strong but Agent B misses a key required legal detail, Agent A wins on completeness and accuracy.

Therefore: A_BETTER (winner more complete; loser missed required sub-point).

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.question: When exploring official online resources related to setting up and using a Mac computer, you can navigate to broader corporate and legal information pages maintained by the same company. Identify the three distinct types of pages that respectively: (1) provide a comprehensive navigational directory of the company’s products, services, retail options, and support resources; (2) outline the company’s United States return, refund, price adjustment, and order policies for hardware purchases; and (3) govern a user’s legal rights, responsibilities, and limitations when accessing and using the company’s main website. For each of these three pages, explain their primary purpose and summarize key provisions, including any specific time limits, monetary thresholds, user obligations, liability caps, governing law clauses, or notable restrictions described on each page.
checklist_width: Comprehensive description of the navigational directory page’s function (broad listing of products, services, retail, support, corporate info)
Examples of categories included in the directory (e.g., devices, services, support, retail programs)
Statement of the standard 14-day return window in the U.S. sales/refund policy
Conditions for returns (original condition, receipt, included parts/accessories)
Details about price adjustment policy within 14 days and exclusions (e.g., limited-time promotions)
Mention of specific refund handling thresholds (e.g., over $750 refund by mailed check)
Reference to wireless carrier cancellation responsibility
Summary of website usage rules (prohibition of scraping, hacking, impersonation, misuse)
Statement of liability limitation (greater of fees paid in prior six months or US$100)
Governing law and venue clause (United States and State of California, Santa Clara County)
checklist_depth: Apple Site Map page identified as the comprehensive navigational directory linked from the main website
U.S. Sales and Refund Policy page identified as the document governing returns, refunds, and price adjustments for U.S. purchases
Terms of Use page identified as the legal agreement governing access to and use of the company’s primary website
rounds.rationale: The question anchors itself in the Mac user journey (starting from official Mac help resources) but requires the respondent to logically navigate to three separate, high-level corporate/legal pages linked from the same ecosystem (Deep reasoning). It then demands aggregation of specific provisions—time limits, dollar thresholds, liability caps, governing law, and usage restrictions—from all three distinct documents (Wide scope), ensuring the answer cannot be derived from a single source.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Apple Site Map About Apple Apple Leadership Career Opportunities Investors Contact Apple Legal Ethics & Compliance Choose Your Country or Region Product Feedback Apple Values
rounds.answer_a:
Apple Sitemap – https://www.apple.com/sitemap/  
Purpose: A purely navigational directory that expands every major section of Apple’s web presence so visitors can jump straight to product families, services, retail programs, corporate pages, and support portals.  
Key provisions: It contains no contractual terms, deadlines, or monetary limits; its relevance is organizational. Products are grouped by hardware line (Mac, iPhone, iPad, Watch, Vision, TV & Home, AirPods), each with “Compare,” “Accessories,” and “Support” links; separate clusters list Services (Music, TV+, iCloud, One), Apple Store options (financing, trade-in, order status), and corporate resources (Leadership, Investor Relations, Jobs) [1].

Apple Sales & Refunds Policy (United States) – https://www.apple.com/shop/help/sales_refunds  
Purpose: Defines the rules for buying hardware from Apple’s U.S. online or retail stores, including returns, refunds, exchanges, shipping, and price adjustments.  
Key provisions:  
• Time limits – Standard return or exchange window is 14 calendar days from the date the customer receives the product; the same 14-day period applies to gift returns and to requesting a price-adjustment refund if Apple drops the price after purchase [2].  
• Monetary thresholds – Refunds are limited to the item’s original purchase price plus applicable tax; Apple may deduct the value of missing accessories or damage. Shipping fees are refunded only when the entire order is returned or Apple shipped in error [2].  
• User obligations & restrictions – Purchases are for end-user use only; Apple may cancel orders it suspects are for resale and may limit quantities. Returned items must be in original packaging with serial number intact [2].

Apple Website Terms of Use – https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html  
Purpose: The binding agreement that governs every visitor’s access to any apple.com domain, covering intellectual-property ownership, acceptable use, dispute resolution, and liability.  
Key provisions:  
• Prohibited behavior – Forbids “robots, spiders, crawlers, scraping tools or other automated means” to access or copy the site, attempts to interfere with servers, and unauthorized access to accounts or systems [3].  
• Liability cap – Apple’s aggregate liability for all damages related to site use is capped at US $100, even if remedies fail of their essential purpose; Apple disclaims indirect, incidental, and consequential damages [3].  
• Governing law & venue – Agreement is governed by California law; any dispute must be brought exclusively in state or federal courts located in Santa Clara County, California, and must be filed within one year of the claim arising [3].  
• Account security – Users must keep Apple ID credentials confidential and notify Apple immediately of unauthorized use; they are responsible for all activities under their account [3].

Collectively, the Sitemap guides discovery, the Sales & Refunds Policy controls post-purchase rights, and the Terms of Use set baseline legal rules for merely browsing or interacting with Apple’s website.

## References  
1. Apple Sitemap – https://www.apple.com/sitemap/  
2. Apple Sales & Refunds Policy (U.S.) – https://www.apple.com/shop/help/sales_refunds  
3. Apple Website Terms of Use – https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html
rounds.answer_b:
**1. Apple Site Map (apple.com/sitemap)** serves as a comprehensive navigational directory organizing Apple's entire web presence into structured categories. It maps out major product families—**Mac, iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, Apple TV, AirPods, and HomePod**—with subcategories for individual models, comparisons, and accessories [1]. Services like **Apple Music, Apple TV+, iCloud, Apple Pay, and Apple Arcade** are grouped separately. Additional sections cover the **Apple Store** (online and retail), **Support** (including user guides, AppleCare, Genius Bar, and Apple Support Communities), **Apple Values** (privacy, accessibility, environment), and account management resources [1]. This page imposes no time limits, monetary provisions, or behavioral restrictions—it functions purely as a navigational aid.

**2. Apple Sales and Refunds Policy (apple.com/shop/help/returns_refund)** governs returns, refunds, price adjustments, and order policies for hardware and accessories purchased through Apple's U.S. online and retail stores. Key provisions include a **14-calendar-day return window** from the date of receipt for most products [2]. Refunds are issued to the original payment method, and items must be returned in original condition. **Opened software, electronic gift cards, and Apple Print Products** are generally non-returnable [2]. Apple reserves the right to refuse returns that fail eligibility criteria. Products are limited to purchase quantities for personal use, restricting **commercial resale**. Apple also offers **price adjustments** if it reduces the price of an Apple-branded product within 14 days of purchase. A **restocking fee** may apply for items not returned in their original condition [2].

**3. Apple Terms of Use (apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html)** is the legal agreement governing all use of Apple's website. It prohibits **automated access** via robots, spiders, scrapers, or similar tools without Apple's prior written permission [3]. Users are solely responsible for maintaining **account credential confidentiality** and must immediately report unauthorized access. Apple's total liability is capped at **$100 USD**, and all indirect, incidental, special, consequential, and punitive damages are disclaimed [3]. The Terms are governed by the laws of the **State of California**, excluding conflict-of-law provisions, with exclusive jurisdiction in **Santa Clara County, California** [3]. Apple retains full intellectual property rights over all site content.

Together, these three pages collectively govern how users **navigate** Apple's ecosystem, **purchase and return** products under defined conditions, and **legally interact** with Apple's digital services.

## References
[1] Apple, "Apple Site Map," https://www.apple.com/sitemap/
[2] Apple, "Returns & Refunds," https://www.apple.com/shop/help/returns_refund
[3] Apple, "Apple Terms of Use," https://www.apple.com/legal/internet-services/terms/site.html
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identified the three required entities—the Apple Site Map, the U.S. Sales & Refunds Policy, and the Apple Website Terms of Use. So both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Both agents included the 14-day return window, return condition requirements, price adjustment window, scraping prohibitions, $100 liability cap, and California/Santa Clara governing law. However, both missed certain checklist nuances (e.g., mailed check threshold over $750, wireless carrier cancellation responsibility, and the "greater of fees paid or $100" liability formulation). That said, Agent B introduced a likely inaccuracy by stating that a restocking fee may apply—Apple’s U.S. policy generally does not impose a standard restocking fee. Agent A avoided this potential hallucination and included additional useful details (e.g., one-year claim limitation, shipping refund conditions, account security obligations).

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both responses are well structured and readable, but Agent A provides slightly richer policy detail and clearer separation of purpose vs. key provisions. While Agent B uses strong bold formatting, the marginal formatting advantage does not outweigh the added nuance and safer accuracy in Agent A’s answer.

Therefore, Agent A is better overall due to slightly stronger factual precision and depth, though both responses are generally high quality.
